The condition is aggravated by
application of the same policy to
operating forces. The man who
could make the road show maxi
mum profit and minimum expendi-
ture, regardless of life or limb or
public demand, shot into the as
cendancy.


The Hill policy of railroading is
VIGOROUSLY a policy of cheap con-
struction and cheap operation. 1t
demands cheap material and cheap
men. Consequently older officials
worth good salaries are let out
whenever there is an opportunity
for doing so. Younger men with
promotion promised, work harder,
work cheaper, and are more sub
sevient. When they arrive at the
age of 40 o thereabout they meet
the fate of their predecessors. That
is tn way their promised promotion
usually pans out Any Hill rail
road man in position to tell the
truth will confirm this.


One result is told in the wrecks
that have become matters of al
most daily occurrence, especially
on the Great Northern. Lives Of
trainmen are sacrificed to this pol
icy continually, but that makes no
difference with dividends. If the
company Is caught where it cannot
escape, as in the Diamond lake ac-
cident, 1t merely denies the fact Of
a wreck and settles on a cash basis
with survivors of its i'll fated en-
ployes. Then the management re-
